data.question: If policy issued at standard rates to healthy applicant, and life insured develops severe illness three years later, can insurance company increase premiums or downgrade risk class?

options.A: Yes, can re-evaluate risk class yearly
options.B: Yes, but must wait until 10-year anniversary
options.C: No, locked in once issued; cannot downgrade or increase premium due to health decline
options.D: No, but can legally cancel policy

data.explanation: Once issued, premiums and risk class cannot be changed due to applicant's health
data.kidExplanation: Once the insurance company shakes your hand and gives you the policy, your health is locked in! If you get really sick later, they are NOT allowed to raise your price or take away your policy. That's why you buy insurance when you are healthy!

data.question: When processing a death claim, insurer discovers life insured honestly misstated age, appearing 5 years younger. How will insurer handle claim?

options.A: Deny claim entirely due to misrepresentation
options.B: Pay full face amount but fine beneficiary
options.C: Pay adjusted death benefit reflecting amount premiums would have purchased at correct age
options.D: Refund premiums to estate and cancel

data.explanation: Age misstatement results in adjusted death benefit to match premium paid
data.kidExplanation: If you accidentally say you are younger than you are, you paid a cheaper price than you were supposed to. The insurance company won't cancel the policy, they just shrink the payout to match the cheaper price you were paying!

data.question: In the "Agent's comments" section of the application, what is the agent expected to do?

options.A: Provide formal medical diagnosis based on observation
options.B: Record observations regarding applicant's health, lifestyle, smoking status that contradict answers

data.explanation: Agent comments document observations that may indicate misrepresentation
data.kidExplanation: If the client checks the box saying 'I never smoke,' but the agent sees them put out a cigarette before the meeting, the agent has to tell the truth in the 'Agent's Comments' box. The agent's job is to protect the insurance company from fibs!

data.question: Michael is in excellent health but frequently travels to a dangerous conflict zone. To avoid declining entirely, underwriter might issue with a specific condition. What is this called?

options.C: An exclusion denying death benefit if dies in that country

data.explanation: Activity-specific exclusions allow issuance while limiting exposure
data.kidExplanation: An 'exclusion' is a special rule that says 'We will cover you for EVERYTHING... except for this one super dangerous thing you like to do.' If you pass away doing that one specific thing, they don't pay.

data.question: Why is agent's delivery of issued policy considered a continuation of underwriting?

options.B: Agent must ensure medical or personal situation hasn't changed since application
options.C: Agent must administer second medical exam
options.D: Agent must verbally read entire contract

data.explanation: Agent confirms applicant's condition hasn't materially changed before policy delivery
data.kidExplanation: Between the time you apply and the time the policy is ready, you might have had a heart attack! When the agent hands you the final policy, they have to double-check that you are still just as healthy as you were when you first applied.

data.question: If policyholder names spouse as "irrevocable beneficiary" on application, what does this mean for future changes?

options.A: Can never change beneficiary under any circumstances
options.B: Can only change if spouse dies
options.C: Cannot change without written permission of spouse
options.D: Can change anytime by paying fee

data.explanation: Irrevocable beneficiary designation requires beneficiary's written consent to change
data.kidExplanation: 'Irrevocable' means locked tight! If you make your spouse the irrevocable beneficiary, you can't ever take them off the policy unless they literally sign a piece of paper saying, 'I agree to let you take me off.'

data.question: Susan applied for $10 million coverage on her husband who earns $45,000 yearly and has no major debts. Underwriter declines. Why?

options.A: Amount not justified; insurance not meant to provide way to profit from death
options.B: Companies forbidden from issuing over $5 million
options.D: Husband must apply for his own insurance

data.explanation: Insurance must be proportional to financial need, not create profit motive
data.kidExplanation: Life insurance is supposed to act as a safety net to replace the money your family lost, not act as a giant lottery ticket to make you super rich! If the husband only makes a little bit of money, asking for 10 million dollars is way too much.

data.question: When underwriter assigns baseline score (e.g., 100), this usually represents what?

options.B: Mortality risk posed by average person same age

data.explanation: Baseline score represents standard mortality risk for age
data.kidExplanation: The underwriter starts everyone at a completely average score of 100. If you are healthier than average, your score drops. If you are sicker than average, your score goes up. It's just a starting point!

data.question: What happens if agent submits application missing answers to several medical questions?

options.C: Underwriter returns to agent to obtain missing information, causing delay

data.explanation: Incomplete applications must be completed before underwriting
data.kidExplanation: If you hand in a test with blank questions, the teacher gives it back to you to finish! The underwriter won't guess your answers, they will mail it back to the agent and you have to wait even longer for your insurance.

data.question: When premiums at issue are based on "attained age" of life insured, how is age typically calculated?

options.A: Always exact age in years, months, and days
options.B: May be age at last birthday, nearest birthday, or next birthday depending on insurer
options.C: Always age turning on December 31st that year

data.explanation: Attained age calculation varies by insurer
data.kidExplanation: Different insurance companies use different clocks! Some look at how old you were on your last birthday, some look at how old you will be on your next birthday, and some just pick the birthday you are closest to today!
